summary: Italian neorealist director and actor (1901–1974). Best known for Bicycle Thieves (1948), Umberto D. (1952), and Shoeshine (Sciuscia, 1946). Frequent scriptwriter collaborator: Cesare Zavattini. Known for using non-professional actors. Andre Bazin was a key critical champion of his work. Also acted in Max Ophuls's The Earrings of Madame de...

works.name: Bicycle Thieves / Ladri di biciclette (1948)
works.description: Antonio Ricci and his son Bruno wander Rome searching for Antonio's stolen bicycle, which he needs to keep his job putting up advertising posters (including one of Rita Hayworth). Maria (Antonio's wife) pawns the family's bedsheets to retrieve the bicycle from a pawnshop. A famous restaurant scene cuts between a rich family enjoying their meal and the poor father-son duo discussing death. The film ends with Bruno crying as Antonio, having attempted to steal a bicycle himself, walks into the crowd. Based on Luigi Bartolini's novel about a bicycle theft. Satyajit Ray's Apu Trilogy was inspired by this film. Inspired parodies including one by Maurizio Nichetti. Andre Bazin praised a scene showing the couple through a small pawnshop window. Wikipedia

works.name: Umberto D. (1952)
works.description: Follows an elderly retired bureaucrat (Umberto D.) who is too proud to beg for rent money. He teaches his dog Flike to beg for him (hat in the dog's mouth on the street). He contemplates jumping in front of a moving train, but his dog whimpers at him. Andre Bazin praised a scene in which a young maid makes coffee and stares out the window at a cat. On returning from tonsillitis treatment, Umberto finds his landlady has knocked a giant hole in his room's wall, leading to his eviction. Screenplay by Cesare Zavattini. Wikipedia

works.name: Shoeshine / Sciuscia (1946)
works.description: An early neorealist film whose two boy protagonists are shoeshine workers who rob a fortune teller to afford to buy a horse. Won the first honorary Oscar for Best International Feature Film (i.e., Best Foreign Language Film). Wikipedia

clues.clue: First winner of the honorary Oscar for Best International Feature Film; two protagonists (shoeshine boys) rob a fortune teller to afford to buy a horse
examples: The first winner of the Oscar for Best International Feature was a film titled for this job [shoeshine] whose two protagonists rob a fortune teller to afford to buy a horse.
De Sica's films Bicycle Thieves and Shoeshine were two of the first three winners of the honorary version of this Academy Award.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Vittorio de Sica (1901–1974) was a pioneer of Italian neorealism. His frequent scriptwriter collaborator was Cesare Zavattini (Bicycle Thieves, Umberto D.). He used non-professional actors. Andre Bazin was his most important critical champion. He was also an actor — he played the diplomat Baron Donati in Max Ophuls's The Earrings of Madame de... (1953). He directed Marriage Italian Style (1964) and Divorce Italian Style-related films with Marcello Mastroianni. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Pioneer of Italian neorealism alongside Roberto Rossellini

comprehensive_summary:
Vittorio de Sica (1901–1974) was the central figure in Italian neorealism, alongside Roberto Rossellini. His most important collaborator was screenwriter Cesare Zavattini, with whom he made Bicycle Thieves and Umberto D., both featuring non-professional actors. Andre Bazin was his most important critical champion.

Bicycle Thieves (Ladri di biciclette, 1948) is his masterpiece. Antonio Ricci and his son Bruno wander Rome in search of Antonio's stolen bicycle — which he needs to hold a job putting up advertising posters (including one of Rita Hayworth). His wife Maria has pawned the family's bedsheets to retrieve the bicycle from a pawnshop. A famous restaurant scene cuts between a rich family and the poor father-son duo discussing death. The film ends with Bruno crying as Antonio, having himself attempted to steal a bicycle, walks into the crowd — a dynamic inversion foreshadowed by an early scene where one character imitates the other's morning routine. Bazin praised a scene showing the couple through a small pawnshop window. The film was adapted from Luigi Bartolini's novel and inspired both Satyajit Ray's Apu Trilogy and parodies by Maurizio Nichetti.

Umberto D. (1952) follows an elderly retired bureaucrat too proud to beg for rent money. He trains his dog Flike to beg (the dog sits on the street with a hat in its mouth). On returning from tonsillitis treatment, Umberto finds his landlady has knocked a giant hole in his room's wall, leading to eviction. He contemplates jumping in front of a train but is stopped by his dog whimpering. Bazin praised a scene in which a young maid makes coffee and stares out the window at a cat.

Shoeshine (Sciuscia, 1946) features two shoeshine-boy protagonists who rob a fortune teller to afford to buy a horse. It won the first honorary Oscar for Best International Feature Film.

De Sica was also a celebrated actor. He played Baron Donati — the diplomat who returns a pair of earrings from Constantinople to Paris — in Max Ophuls's The Earrings of Madame de... (1953).

cards.front: Film: Antonio Ricci and son Bruno wander Rome looking for a stolen bicycle needed for his job putting up Rita Hayworth posters; his wife pawned the family's bedsheets to retrieve it
Film: a restaurant scene cuts between a rich family and the poor father-son duo discussing death
Film: ends with Bruno crying as Antonio walks into the crowd after attempting to steal a bicycle himself; their roles are inverted
Film: inspired Satyajit Ray's Apu Trilogy; based on Luigi Bartolini's novel; parodies by Maurizio Nichetti
Film: elderly Umberto D. is too proud to beg; trains his dog Flike to sit with a hat in its mouth to beg passersby for rent money
Film: Umberto contemplates jumping in front of a train but is stopped by his dog whimpering; returns from tonsillitis to find his landlady has knocked a hole in his room's wall
Film: two shoeshine-boy protagonists rob a fortune teller to buy a horse; first winner of the honorary Oscar for Best International Feature Film
Director: frequent scriptwriter collaborator was Cesare Zavattini; used non-professional actors; pioneer of Italian neorealism alongside Rossellini
Director: also acted in Max Ophuls's The Earrings of Madame de... as Baron Donati, the diplomat who returns earrings from Constantinople to Paris

recursive_suggestions: Bicycle Thieves — rich standalone clue set; the film itself (not just de Sica) frequently appears as an answerline
Italian Neorealism — broader movement worth a standalone search
Roberto Rossellini — neorealism co-pioneer; also associated with Ingrid Bergman
